description:
When mortals die in Greek mythology, their psyche (soul/shade) descends to the Underworld ruled by Hades
                and Persephone. The journey involves crossing treacherous rivers, facing judgment, and eternal residence
                in one of several realms based on one's life. Unlike many traditions, most souls face neither eternal bliss
                nor torment, but rather a dim existence in the Asphodel Meadows.

elysium.also: Elysian Fields, Islands of the Blessed

tartarus.depth: As far below Hades as heaven is above earth

asphodel.experience: Dim, shadowy existence - neither joy nor torment

name: The Journey to Hades

keyMyths.title: Charon's Ferry
keyMyths.description: The deceased must pay Charon, the ferryman of the River Styx or Acheron, with an obolus (coin) for passage to the Underworld. Those without the fare, or unburied, were doomed to wander the shores for eternity.

keyMyths.title: Cerberus, the Guardian
keyMyths.description: Cerberus, the multi-headed hound, guards the entrance to Hades, preventing the living from entering and the dead from leaving. He is often depicted with three heads, a serpent tail, and a mane of snakes.

keyMyths.title: Judgment by the Judges
keyMyths.description: Upon arrival, souls are judged by Minos, Rhadamanthus, and Aeacus, who determine their destination based on their deeds in life. The particularly virtuous are sent to Elysium, while the wicked are punished in Tartarus.

keyMyths.title: The Torments of Tartarus
keyMyths.description: Tartarus is a deep, dark abyss reserved for the wicked and those who offended the gods. Famous figures like Tantalus, Sisyphus, and Ixion suffer eternal punishments for their transgressions.

keyMyths.title: Orpheus and Eurydice
keyMyths.description: Orpheus, a skilled musician, journeyed to the Underworld to retrieve his deceased wife, Eurydice. He charmed Hades and Persephone with his music, but lost her again when he disobeyed their condition to not look back at her until they reached the upper world.

sources.name: Homer, *Odyssey*
sources.description: An epic poem recounting Odysseus's journey home, including his descent to the Underworld (Nekyia) in Book 11.
sources.title: Homer, *Odyssey*

sources.name: Virgil, *Aeneid*
sources.description: A Latin epic poem that describes Aeneas's journey to the Underworld in Book 6.
sources.title: Virgil, *Aeneid*

sources.name: Hesiod, *Theogony*
sources.description: A poem detailing the origins of the gods and the cosmos, including descriptions of Tartarus and the Underworld.
sources.title: Hesiod, *Theogony*

sources.name: Plato, *Gorgias*
sources.description: A philosophical dialogue that discusses the afterlife and the judgment of souls.
sources.title: Plato, *Gorgias*

sources.name: Ovid, *Metamorphoses*
sources.description: A narrative poem recounting various myths, including the story of Orpheus and Eurydice's journey to and from the Underworld.
sources.title: Ovid, *Metamorphoses*
